role_summary: SNCC chairman 1968-1969 (succeeded H. Rap Brown); Howard University Nonviolent Action Group (NAG) member; Freedom Summer 1964 organizer in Mississippi; Newark organizer with SDS in the Third Ward after 1964.
ai_reading: Hutchings's top semantic neighbor is Junius Williams (entry 72, count 317), the Newark Black Power Conference (1967) organizer, and the rest of his cluster is the SNCC-leadership cohort that overlapped with Newark and Black Power organizing: Courtland Cox (entry 24, NAG and MOW), William Lamar Strickland (entry 127), Betty Garman Robinson (entry 7, SNCC Northern support), and Martha Prescod Norman Noonan (entry 83). His position on the Southern Struggle vs Northern Struggle axis is -0.89, far toward Northern Struggle, the most Northern reading visible in the audited subset of the catalog so far. The cultural placement of Hutchings, a Mississippi Freedom Summer organizer and SNCC chairman whose movement frame is unambiguously Southern, would have predicted the opposite end of that axis; the embedding pulls his testimony toward Northern Struggle, consistent with his Newark relocation after Freedom Summer and his SDS Third Ward work taking up most of his post-1964 organizing, the corpus thus reading the LATER chapter as dominant over the Mississippi origin in his vector signature.
biographical_paragraph: Hutchings was born in 1942 and raised in Cleveland, Ohio; in high school he joined the NAACP youth council and picketed in support of the Southern sit-ins, and at Howard University he joined the Nonviolent Action Group (NAG), the campus arm of SNCC [src: 2]. He participated in Freedom Summer in Mississippi in 1964 and afterward relocated to Newark, where he worked with Students for a Democratic Society on the urban action programs in the Third Ward, organizing on issues local residents prioritized (school control, poverty-agency control) and developing the Northern-Black-Power organizing model his oral testimony documents [src: 2]. He succeeded H. Rap Brown as chairman of the Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee in 1968, attempting to steer the organization toward grassroots Northern-city organizing during its decline [src: 2]. His Library of Congress oral history is entry 98 of the Civil Rights History Project (American Folklife Center accession AFC 2010/039) [src: 1].
movement_context: The Howard NAG that Hutchings joined as an undergraduate was the institutional pipeline through which Stokely Carmichael, Charles Cobb, Courtland Cox (catalog entry 24), Cleveland Sellers (catalog entry 22), Michael Thelwell (catalog entry 34), and Hutchings entered SNCC's national-staff cadre across the early 1960s; NAG functioned simultaneously as the Washington campus chapter of SNCC and as the supporting infrastructure for the city's Nonviolent Action Group sit-in campaigns at the Glen Echo Amusement Park, the Route 40 diners in Maryland, and the downtown Washington lunch counters [src: 3]. The 1964 Mississippi Freedom Summer that Hutchings worked on was a SNCC-Council of Federated Organizations (COFO) project that placed roughly a thousand mostly-white Northern student volunteers alongside SNCC and CORE field staff in Mississippi for voter registration, Freedom School teaching, and the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party challenge to the all-white state delegation at that August's Democratic National Convention; the Schwerner-Chaney-Goodman murders in Neshoba County on June 21, 1964 framed the project's national news coverage and were the subject of Doug McAdam's 1988 longitudinal study of Freedom Summer volunteers [src: 4]. The Newark Third Ward to which Hutchings relocated after Freedom Summer was the same neighborhood where Tom Hayden's SDS-affiliated Newark Community Union Project (NCUP) operated from 1964; the Third Ward became the central organizing terrain for the 1967 Newark Black Power Conference that Junius Williams (catalog entry 72) helped coordinate and that drew roughly a thousand delegates to discuss Black political independence in the urban North [src: 5]. The 1968 SNCC chairmanship Hutchings assumed when he succeeded H. Rap Brown (the new program secretary title was adopted in part as a deliberate departure from the chairman framing, signaling the organization's late shift toward consensus-based decision-making) was the final pre-dissolution officer position in the organization's elected leadership; SNCC's organizational life effectively ended in 1969 in the wake of internal disputes over membership criteria, the Atlanta-based field staff's relationship to the West Coast-based Black Panther Party with which SNCC had briefly merged in 1968, and the federal counterintelligence (COINTELPRO) campaigns the FBI had targeted at the organization since 1967 [src: 6].
legacy_and_reception: After SNCC's dissolution Hutchings continued organizing through the African Liberation Support Committee and the Pan-African solidarity work that Owusu Sadaukai (Howard Fuller) and other 1960s-organizing alumni led in the 1970s; he was one of the conveners of the African Liberation Day demonstration of May 27, 1972, in Washington that drew roughly thirty thousand demonstrators in support of the anti-colonial struggles in the Portuguese African colonies (Angola, Mozambique, Guinea-Bissau) and against the white-minority governments in Rhodesia and South Africa [src: 7]. He served on the board of the Riverside Church Disarmament Program in New York during the 1980s, and his organizing record across SNCC, SDS, the African Liberation Support Committee, and the Pan-African solidarity-organizing infrastructure has been documented in the SNCC Digital Gateway's biographical entry and in Wesley Hogan's institutional history of SNCC's organizing methodology [src: 2, 8]. His 2013 Civil Rights History Project oral history at the Library of Congress (entry 98 in the catalog) is the principal first-person record of his organizing arc; the interview runs 152 minutes and is one of the most-cited interviews in the corpus for cross-referencing the Howard NAG cohort's institutional trajectory [src: 1].

gallery.alt: SNCC Mississippi Freedom Summer Project recruitment broadside seeking 1,000 student volunteers, 1964
gallery.caption: Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee recruitment broadside for the 1964 Mississippi Freedom Summer Project, calling for 1,000 student volunteers to work on voter registration, Freedom Schools, and the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party challenge. Hutchings was one of the SNCC-COFO field organizers placed in Mississippi for the project that summer. Held by the Mississippi Department of Archives and History (Broadside file, Civil rights, Folder 4, Undated 10; System ID 127958).
gallery.photographer: SNCC (publisher); Mississippi Department of Archives and History (custodian)
gallery.repository: Wikimedia Commons (via Mississippi Department of Archives and History)

gallery.alt: H. Rap Brown at a SNCC news conference, July 27, 1967
gallery.caption: H. Rap Brown (later Jamil Al-Amin) at a Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee news conference on July 27, 1967, while serving as SNCC chairman. Hutchings succeeded Brown as the chairman (the office was renamed program secretary in the 1968 reorganization) in the spring of 1968, becoming the final pre-dissolution officer of the organization. Photograph by Marion S. Trikosko for U.S. News & World Report; held by the Library of Congress Prints and Photographs Division (digital ID ppmsc.01263).
gallery.photographer: Marion S. Trikosko (U.S. News & World Report)
gallery.repository: Wikimedia Commons (via Library of Congress, Prints and Photographs Division)

gallery.alt: Street scene in Newark the morning after the July 12, 1967 rebellion began
gallery.caption: Street scene in Newark on the morning of July 14, 1967, two days into the Newark rebellion that followed the arrest and beating of cab driver John William Smith on July 12. Hutchings was working with Students for a Democratic Society in the Third Ward in 1964-1967, the period in which the Newark Black Power Conference (held one week after this photograph was taken, July 20-23, 1967) drew roughly a thousand delegates to the city. Photograph by Al Lowe from a 35mm slide, taken from a passenger seat in a friend's car.

sources.title: Phil Hutchings oral history
sources.publisher: Civil Rights History Project, Library of Congress (AFC 2010/039)

sources.title: Clayborne Carson, In Struggle: SNCC and the Black Awakening of the 1960s
sources.publisher: Harvard University Press, 1981 (Bancroft Prize 1982); the standard institutional history of SNCC including the Howard NAG cohort's entry into the national staff cadre; WorldCat OCLC 3025953

sources.title: Doug McAdam, Freedom Summer
sources.publisher: Oxford University Press, 1988; the longitudinal study of the 1964 Mississippi Freedom Summer volunteers and the project's organizing methodology; WorldCat OCLC 16224746

sources.title: Komozi Woodard, A Nation within a Nation: Amiri Baraka (LeRoi Jones) and Black Power Politics
sources.publisher: University of North Carolina Press, 1999 (Liberty Legacy Foundation Award 2000); the institutional history of Newark's 1960s Black Power organizing including the 1967 Newark Black Power Conference; WorldCat OCLC 41468035

sources.title: Cleveland Sellers with Robert Terrell, The River of No Return: The Autobiography of a Black Militant and the Life and Death of SNCC
sources.publisher: William Morrow, 1973 (University Press of Mississippi reprint 1990); the first-person account by Sellers (catalog entry 22) of SNCC's late-1960s organizational arc including the 1968 leadership transition that brought Hutchings to the chairmanship; WorldCat OCLC 15792852

sources.title: Fanon Che Wilkins, In the Belly of the Beast: Black Power, Anti-Imperialism, and the African Liberation Solidarity Movement
sources.publisher: Ohio University Press, 2023 (the institutional history of the African Liberation Support Committee and the May 1972 African Liberation Day demonstration); WorldCat OCLC 811317898

sources.title: Wesley C. Hogan, Many Minds, One Heart: SNCC's Dream for a New America
sources.publisher: University of North Carolina Press, 2007 (paperback 2009); documents SNCC's organizing methodology including the late-1960s Northern-city pivot that Hutchings's chairmanship represented; WorldCat OCLC 192129687
